text.title: Journey Mapping Process
text.summary: Seven-step mapping process from scope definition to visualization, with rules for research grounding and emotional arc.

rule.statement: Map one persona and one journey per map; scope to a specific start and end point.
rule.rationale: Combining two personas hides differences between their experiences, producing a map that represents neither accurately. A scope without defined endpoints produces maps with no clear beginning or resolution.

example.note: Scope: Sarah (mid-market buyer persona), purchase journey from first product discovery to post-purchase unboxing. Start: first ad impression. End: product first use.

rule.statement: Collect multi-source evidence before mapping: user interviews, observation, analytics, support tickets, surveys, and employee interviews from customer-facing roles.
rule.rationale: Single-source maps are advocacy documents masquerading as research. Customer support staff see pain points daily that UX research misses — their input is mandatory, not optional.

rule.statement: Break the journey into 4-8 major stages; name stages from the user's perspective, not the company's process names.
rule.rationale: Company process names (Lead, Opportunity, Closed) reflect internal pipeline stages, not user experience stages. User-perspective names (Discovering, Evaluating, Deciding) keep the map focused on user experience, not operations.

rule.statement: Map emotions with grounding in research evidence; never invent emotional states from team assumptions.
rule.rationale: Invented emotional arcs produce maps that validate team assumptions rather than challenge them. LLMs and teams both smooth emotional arcs — real journeys have sharp dips. Prompt explicitly for variance when using agents.

section.title: Step 6 — Identify Pain Points and Opportunities

rule.statement: The emotional arc dip is the primary signal for where to invest redesign effort; prioritize pain points at the sharpest drops.
rule.rationale: Sharp emotional dips represent the highest concentration of user frustration. These stages have the most to gain from improvement and the most risk from neglect.

rule.statement: Every opportunity in the Opportunities column must link to a backlog item or hypothesis — a map without downstream action is waste.
rule.rationale: Journey maps created without action commitments become deck artifacts. The opportunities column is only valuable if it generates work.

rule.statement: Attach a "last validated" date to every map; trigger a review when a major feature ships that affects the mapped journey.
rule.rationale: Journey maps go stale quickly. A map from last quarter may contradict current analytics after a product change. Stale maps create false consensus about a user experience that no longer exists.
